---
title: "Using AI Tools for R Coding"
description: "How to use AI assistants deliberately and critically in R programming — understanding what AI can and cannot do, three modes of effective use, the failure modes to watch for, and the one habit that makes AI assistance durable."
author: "Claudius Gräbner-Radkowitsch"
date: 2026-06-16
date-modified: 2026-06-16
image: ../../assets/images/placeholder.png

learning-objectives:
  - "Identify tasks where AI assistance adds value in R programming and where it does not"
  - "Apply the three modes of AI use (autocomplete, thought partner, code reviewer) deliberately"
  - "Recognise and diagnose the three common AI failure modes: package hallucination, stale API knowledge, and vibe coding"
  - "Apply the prompt → inspect → run → diagnose habit consistently in your workflow"

categories:
  - R
  - artificial-intelligence
  - BA
  - MA
  - tutorial

level:
  - BA
  - MA

content-type: tutorial

citation:
  type: entry
  container-title: "Methods Hub"
  title: "Using AI Tools for R Coding"
  editor:
    - name: "Claudius Gräbner-Radkowitsch"
  url: https://euf-methodology-hub.netlify.app/resources/r-programming/using-ai-for-r-coding
---

Modern AI tools can meaningfully accelerate R programming work — but only if you already
understand the language well enough to evaluate what the AI produces. An AI that generates
R code you cannot read is not a shortcut; it is a liability.

Understanding what AI is actually doing helps. AI predicts plausible next tokens given
its training data. It does not reason about your problem, look things up, or know what has
changed since its training cutoff. Confidence is not correctness — fluent-looking code is
not correct code. This has direct consequences for how you should use it.

# Video lecture {.unnumbered}

```{=html}
<div class="embed-container">
  <iframe
    src="https://gist.github.com/graebnerc/5bf7f47a92afc22beb63c6fbc1d30831.js"
    style="border: 0.5px"
    width="80%" height="80%">
  </iframe>
</div>
```

::: {.callout-tip}
## Prompts and code examples from the lecture
<script src="https://gist.github.com/graebnerc/5bf7f47a92afc22beb63c6fbc1d30831.js"></script>
:::

# Three modes of use

AI assistance is most effective when you choose your mode deliberately. There are three
distinct modes, each with a different risk–gain profile.

**Autocomplete** — AI completes boilerplate you already know how to write. Risk is low,
gain is modest. Use this when you would have typed it anyway.

**Thought partner** — Ask for options, strategies, or chart recommendations — you decide
what to act on. Risk is medium, gain can be high. Works best when you have enough domain
knowledge to evaluate what comes back.

**Code reviewer** — Paste your own code and ask what is wrong or what a careful reader
would criticise. Risk is low, gain is very high. This is among the most useful and
underused modes of AI assistance.

The choice of mode is itself the skill. Modes also combine: use *thought partner* to
sketch a plan, correct it yourself, then *autocomplete* fills in the code. Use *code
reviewer* afterwards to catch what you missed.

# Three failure modes

Knowing where AI typically fails protects you from the most common traps.

**Package hallucination.** AI invents plausible-sounding packages or functions that do
not exist. A 2025 study across 16 models found roughly 20% of recommended packages were
hallucinations. In R, this failure is silent until `library()` throws an error — there is
no warning that the package was invented.

**Stale API knowledge.** AI was trained on older code and cannot know what has changed
since. A common example: the `size` argument in ggplot2 was renamed `linewidth` in
version 3.4.0. The model will confidently emit the old syntax. Deprecated arguments,
renamed functions, moved packages — these failures look identical to correct code until
you run it.

**Vibe coding.** Accepting AI output without reading it, then re-prompting when it breaks
without diagnosing why. Each unread iteration compounds the confusion. This failure mode
is independent of the tool you use; it is a habit failure.

# The invariant habit

One habit makes AI use durable as tools change:

```
prompt  →  inspect  →  run  →  diagnose
   ↑                              │
   └──────────────────────────────┘
```

**Inspect** — read the code *before* running it. Does it do what you asked? Are the
function names real? Does the logic match your data structure?

**Diagnose** — when it breaks, understand *why* before re-prompting. If you paste the
error back without understanding it, you are asking the AI to fix something neither of
you understands.

Skipping *inspect* and *diagnose* is vibe coding, even without that label. Everything
else about these tools may be obsolete in two years. This habit will not be.

# Using AI with Quarto

AI assistants are particularly useful for three Quarto-specific tasks.

**Error diagnosis.** Paste a render error with the surrounding chunk:
> "What is causing this error?"

This is reliable on YAML errors and chunk option problems — the error messages tend to be
syntactically well-defined and the model has seen many examples.

**Goal-finding.** Ask how to achieve a specific Quarto output:
> "How do I make a figure span the full slide width in Reveal.js?"

Use AI here as a documentation shortcut — but always verify at quarto.org before relying
on the answer.

**Prose refinement.** Describe your figure and ask whether the claim holds:
> "Is this claim supported by the figure? Can it be more concise?"

Most tools accept a screenshot. If yours does not, describing the figure forces the
clarity the caption needs anyway.

::: {.callout-note .level-advanced}
## MA / PhD

In data visualisation work, mapping AI strengths and limits explicitly prevents
frustration late in a project.

**AI is strong on:** scaffolding a first draft, explaining error messages, adjusting
individual aesthetic parameters, refactoring code to use the pipe.

**AI struggles with:** choosing the right chart for the question, multi-panel composition
and shared legends (e.g., with `patchwork`), keeping up with the current ggplot2 API
(`linewidth` vs. `size`), journal-specific constraints it was not told about, and
self-correcting when a hallucinated package fails.

AI can get you to roughly 70% of a publication-ready figure. The last 30% requires
judgment you cannot delegate.
:::

# Tools and resources

**Learning to prompt effectively**

- [Anthropic's Prompt Engineering Interactive Tutorial](https://github.com/anthropics/prompt-eng-interactive-tutorial) — a thorough, hands-on course on writing good prompts

**Integrating AI into RStudio**

- [GitHub Copilot in RStudio](https://docs.posit.co/ide/user/ide/guide/tools/copilot.html) — tab-completion and inline suggestions directly in the editor; free for students via [GitHub Education](https://github.com/education/students)
- [Gemini Code Assist](https://codeassist.google) — free tier, but no direct RStudio plugin

**Standalone AI coding environments**

- [Claude Code](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/claude-code/overview) — best suited for larger programming projects rather than single-file R scripts
- [Windsurf](https://windsurf.com) — has a generous free tier but no RStudio integration

For R-specific questions, prefer tools with web search enabled — they are less likely to
emit stale package syntax.

::: {.callout-note}
## A note on reliance

AI tools improve fastest when you treat their output critically. If you paste code you
do not understand into your script, you will struggle to adapt it, debug it, or explain
it — all of which you will eventually need to do. Use AI as an accelerator, not a
replacement for understanding.
:::
